title: Q&A: Thomas Traumann on Brazil’s October Elections
gist: Political analyst Thomas Traumann argues Brazil's 2026 election will be decided by deep Lula-Flávio Bolsonaro polarization, inflation and U.S. tariff effects on employment, and an evolving religious/demographic electorate, with a likely institutional clash between the Senate and Supreme Court over judicial impeachment regardless of who wins.

insights.claim: Traumann argues the top issue in Brazil's 2026 election is polarization between Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva and Flávio Bolsonaro, which grew from 2018 to 2022 and remains extremely powerful, alongside public security, corruption, inflation, U.S. relations, and Lula's health.
insights.speaker: Thomas Traumann
insights.so_what: Extreme, entrenched polarization means the election outcome will likely hinge on turnout/mobilization dynamics rather than persuasion of committed partisans.
insights.locator: The most important thing is polarization

insights.claim: Traumann says if inflation is high, Lula will be punished at the polls, and if U.S. tariffs threaten Brazilian unemployment, that will hurt Flávio Bolsonaro; undecided independent voters will decide in the final weeks based on feeling rather than policy agendas, essentially choosing 'which candidate they hate the least.'
insights.speaker: Thomas Traumann
insights.so_what: Two concrete, trackable macro variables — inflation and U.S. tariff-driven unemployment — are identified as the swing-vote triggers to watch ahead of the October election.
insights.quote: They are not really looking at the big agendas, but rather at which candidate they hate the least.
insights.locator: Independents will decide in the last few weeks

insights.claim: Over the last 20 years, more women have voted and the Brazilian electorate has become older and more educated; evangelicals mostly vote right and Catholics mostly vote left.
insights.speaker: Thomas Traumann
insights.so_what: Long-run demographic shifts (aging, education, female turnout) are structural factors reshaping Brazilian electoral coalitions beyond any single election cycle.
insights.locator: Over the last 20 years, more women have been voting

insights.claim: New census data (delayed by COVID-19) shows evangelicals reached only 27% of Brazil's population versus an expected 30%+, with Catholics at 57%; many attribute the shortfall to evangelical leaders' close ties to bolsonarismo driving some followers away.
insights.speaker: Thomas Traumann
insights.so_what: Evangelical population growth undershooting expectations, linked to political over-association with Bolsonarismo, is a data point that could cap the ceiling for right-wing religious-vote mobilization.
insights.locator: People expected evangelicals would be more than 30%

insights.claim: Traumann predicts that after the 2026 election, six or seven parties could hold over 80% of the lower house's representatives, continuing a decade-long trend of fewer, bigger, stronger parties.
insights.speaker: Thomas Traumann
insights.so_what: Party consolidation in the lower house could make coalition-building more predictable and potentially strengthen governability for whoever wins the presidency.
insights.locator: There have been fewer parties in the lower house

insights.claim: Two-thirds of the Senate will be elected in 2026, and if the right wins a majority, it could gain enough votes to impeach Supreme Court judges; Traumann predicts an institutional clash between the Senate and Supreme Court over judicial impeachment regardless of who becomes president.
insights.speaker: Thomas Traumann
insights.so_what: A Senate-Supreme Court confrontation over judicial impeachment is flagged as a near-certain 2027 institutional risk event independent of the presidential outcome — a governance/rule-of-law risk to monitor for Brazil exposure.
insights.quote: No matter who the president is next year, there will be an institutional clash between the Senate and the Supreme Court over the impeachment of judges.
insights.locator: Two-thirds of the Senate will be elected this year
